Defendant Kitsia Guardado'S Motion To Set Aside Default Judgment, Ccp 473 (B), Ccp 473.5, Civ 1788.61, Ccp 473(D) And Quash Service Of Summons
Off Calendar Mar-11-2020 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT KITSIA GUARDADO'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT JUDGMENT, CCP 473 (B), CCP 473.5, Civ 1788.61, CCP 473(D) And Quash Service Of Summons is adopted as follows:

Defendant Kitsia Guardado's motion to vacate the default of May 5, 2008 and default judgment of May 8, 2008 is denied. Defendant fails to maintain her burden under any of the cited bases for relief.

Defendant is not entitled to relief under Code of Civil Procedure 473.5 because the motion is untimely. Code of Civil Procedure 473.5(a) provides: "The notice of motion shall be served and filed within a reasonable time, but in no event exceeding the earlier of: (i) two years after entry of a default judgment against him or her; or (ii) 180 days after service on him or her of a written notice that the default or default judgment has been entered." Defendant filed the instant application approximately a decade too late.

Defendant is not entitled to relief under Civil Code 1788.61. That provision applies to creditors who purchase consumer debt. (See Civil Code 1788.50 et seq.) It is inapposite to this commercial financing transaction between plaintiff and defendants. In addition, the application is untimely under Civil Code 1788.61(a)(2).

Defendant is not entitled to relief under Code of Civil Procedure 473(d) [void for improper service]. First, the application under this section is untimely because the default judgment is not void on its face and therefore, the timing provisions of Code of Civil Procedure 473.5 apply. (See Trackman v. Kenney (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 176, 180 ["Where a party moves under section 473, subdivision (d) to set aside 'a judgment that, though valid on its face, is void for lack of proper service, the courts have adopted by analogy the statutory period for relief from a default judgment' provided by section 473.5, that is, the two-year outer limit."]; Rogers v. Silverman (1989) 216 Cal.App.3d 114, 1121-1122.) Second, defendant does not convincingly establish that the service was improper. Defendant contends that the substitute service at 4862 Trailhill Court, Riverside CA was improper because at the time of service, her ex-husband owned the property and she was living in Georgia. (Guardodo Dec., e).) That assertion lacks credibility given that defendant has relied on the Trailhill address as her own during the course of this litigation. That address is even listed on defendant's instant motion.

Defendant is not entitled to equitable relief. The court can grant such relief where the movant has a meritorious case, a satisfactory excuse for not presenting a defense to the original action, and diligence in seeking relief once she discovered the default. (See Moghaddam v. Bone (2006) 142 Cal.App.4th 283, 290-291; Rappleyea v. Campbell (1994) 8 Cal.4th 975, 982.) In this case, defendant has not brought the motion in a timely fashion. Plaintiff presents evidence that defendant knew of the action over a decade ago because she proffered a settlement offer through a surrogate in March of 2008. (Dalesky Decl., Ex.4.) Indeed, although plaintiff claimed that she "first learned about this lawsuit on or around May 2018" (Guardado Decl. j), she specifically listed the lawsuit, by case name and number, as well as the associated debt, in schedules attached to a bankruptcy petition she signed and filed in March 2009. Defendant not only has failed to act diligently, she has lied under oath, which categorically disqualifies her from any claim to equitable relief. Defendant also fails to present evidence of a meritorious defense.

The Court signed the proposed order submitted by email.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported.

RULING - The matter is Uncontested. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT KITSIA GUARDADO's MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFULT JUDGMENT, CCP 473(B), CCP 473.5, CIV. 1788.61, CCP 473(D) AND QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS (CCP 418.10)

Default Prove-Up Hearing.
Off Calendar May-08-2008 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: This cause came on regularly as scheduled this day for Default Prove-Up Hearing.
Dept. 218, Comm. William R. Gargano, Clerk: Kevin Dougherty, CSR: Lois Jones, #3689.

Douglas Holloway, Esq. appeared for plaintiff Bank of the Best.
No appearance by defendants Kitsia's Transports Inc. or Kitsia Guardado.
Defaults entered against each defendant on March 5, 2008.
Doe defendants dismissed on March 5, 2008.
Order waiving witness appearance at default prove-up hearing was filed on April 3, 2008.

After hearing the evidence presented, and good cause appearing, the Court rules in favor of Plaintiff Bank of the West, and against the Defaulted Defendants KITSIA'S TRANSPORTS INC. and KITSIA GUARDADA as follows:

Damages/Principal - $169,336.48.
Prejudgment Interest - $8,118.25.
Attorney's Fees - $2,500.00.
Costs - $605.00, for a total of $180,559.73.

It further Ordered that plaintiff Bank of the West is the owner of the following leased equipment: one 2002 Freightliner Comlumbia VJN# 1FUJA6CG121LJ12328; one 2002 Freightliner Columbia VJN# 1FUJA6CG32LJ12279; one 2007 Trailer W56 VJN# 15XFW53367L001554; five 1999 Wabash Reefer Trailers, VIN## 1JJV532W0XL530396, 1JJV532W0XL530401, 1JJV532W8XL530405, 1JJV532W9XL530400, 1JJV532W6XL530404, and is entitled to its immediate possession, and that plaintiff have and recover said property from defendants.

Judgment was submitted to and signed by the Court.
